The investigation isolated the external-login failure to the perimeter Reverse Proxy / WAF rather than the backend application, with tests showing direct-IP access to the reverse proxy worked while FQDN requests proxied via the normal path returned HTTP 502. Analysis showed that enabling TLS inspection (a WAF feature) correlated with the 502 behavior and that disabling TLS inspection temporarily allowed logins, implicating the proxy's TLS-inspection or header-forwarding behavior.

Further evidence traced the behavior to a reload of the WAF default profile after a patch — the profile-level change altered TLS inspection handling and resulted in the X-Forwarded-Proto header not being forwarded (or not being preserved correctly) to the backend. Because the backend relies on X-Forwarded-Proto: https to determine the original protocol, the missing/modified header caused the backend to reject proxied requests and return 502 responses. This chain of events points to a configuration/profile-level regression introduced during patching rather than an application-layer fault. [Data: Entities (637,636,648); Relationships (1211,1227,1210)]

## Operational impact: high-priority external outage affecting customer-facing logins

The support incident was opened as TI-1594408 for Customer Company 40 AG when external users could not log into the customer portal; the ticket priority was High and the customer contact reported contextual changes (a new certificate and a recent firewall patch). External customers were impacted while internal access remained functional, increasing business-impact visibility and customer concern.

The incident progressed through first-line ownership to specialists and concluded with a closure note indicating configuration was adjusted per architectural specifications and access was restored. This confirms both the customer-visible impact and the operational closure state. [Data: Entities (630,631,632,636); Relationships (1197,1198)]

## Backend availability validated by load-balancer health checks, isolating fault to the edge path

Operational checks showed that the load balancer reported the backend server(s) as 'Healthy' via its health checks and that the load balancer successfully routed requests to the backend during tests. This evidence helped narrow the fault domain to the edge reverse-proxy/WAF path rather than application availability or server-side failures.

Because the load balancer performed health checks and maintained session persistence (sticky sessions) while the backend responded, the troubleshooting focus correctly shifted to header forwarding and TLS offload behavior at the perimeter proxy. This separation of roles (edge WAF vs. load balancer vs. backend) was instrumental in finding a configuration-based root cause. [Data: Entities (640,651); Relationships (1222,1223)]

## Change event: security patch caused default WAF profile reload and configuration drift

The sequence of events includes a security patch that resulted in the WAF's default profile being reloaded. That reload altered policy and TLS-inspection behavior at the edge and introduced a deviation from the expected secure-publishing configuration defined in the internal blueprint. The profile reload was a pivotal change that precipitated the outage by modifying how TLS inspection and header handling were performed.

This is a classic example of a patch-induced configuration drift where an automated or operator-triggered reload of a default profile reverts or changes operational settings that downstream services depend upon (in this case header preservation). The timeline and the WAF default profile reload evidence support this causal link. [Data: Entities (648,637); Relationships (1227,1211)]

## Governance and runbook gap: blueprint existed but enforcement/testing around patches was insufficient

Investigators referenced the 'Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3' blueprint as the source of truth and ultimately adjusted the WAF configuration to match the architectural specification, which restored function. The presence and use of the blueprint demonstrates that standards exist; however, the incident shows insufficient pre/post-patch verification, template enforcement, or automated configuration governance to prevent or detect profile reload-induced drift before customer impact.

Specifically, the portal must be published using the Portal-App-Template and preserve headers and TLS offload semantics; the outage occurred because those guarantees were not preserved during the patch/profile reload. Strengthening policy-as-code, automated compliance checks against the blueprint, and a mandatory patch rollback/test sequence would reduce recurrence. [Data: Entities (646,636); Relationships (1205,1210)]

## Roles and escalation: clear escalation to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 enabled resolution

The ticket was opened by first-line staff (Employee 29) and escalated to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 for deeper analysis of the WAF/reverse-proxy and TLS inspection behavior. System Engineer 21 performed the policy/TLS-inspection analysis, ran the tests that demonstrated disabling TLS inspection allowed logins, compared live configuration to the blueprint, and participated in corrective changes.

This handoff path — first-line → BU Network & Security → named second-line engineer — worked effectively to apply specialist knowledge and the blueprint to remediate the issue. The documented escalation chain and personnel involvement provide a workable model to codify as an escalation runbook for similar perimeter/edge incidents. [Data: Entities (633,635,634); Relationships (1199,1201,1204)]

## Recommended prioritized actions: perimeter configuration hardening, pre/post-patch checks, and improved observability

Short-term (high priority): 1) Enforce Portal-App-Template and explicit preservation of X-Forwarded-Proto and related proxy headers in WAF profiles; 2) Add a pre-patch checklist that validates non-default profile settings will persist and post-patch smoke tests including an external login test (synthetic transaction) and header-preservation verification; 3) Implement an immediate alert that detects systematic loss or modification of X-Forwarded-Proto in reverse-proxy logs.

Medium-term: 1) Implement automated configuration compliance (policy-as-code) against the Secure Web Publishing blueprint so profile reloads that deviate are flagged or blocked; 2) Expand CI/CD-style change controls for edge devices (including a staged canary patching approach for WAFs); 3) Update runbooks to include explicit steps for rollback and customer communication on perimeter profile changes. These actions are directly tied to the incident evidence showing a default-profile reload and header-handling change caused customer impact and that blueprint-aligned configuration fixes resolved it. [Data: Entities (648,646,637); Relationships (1227,1205,1210)]

## Customer communication and post-incident tasks to close the loop and reduce recurrence

The customer contact (Contact Person 19) reported the issue and later received closure communication explaining that the reverse-proxy configuration deviation after a security patch caused the failure and that service was restored. Formalizing this practice — immediate interim communications, status updates during specialists’ analysis, and a post-incident report — will improve transparency and trust.

Post-incident actions should include a documented incident RCA referencing the blueprint, inventory of which WAF/profile settings were changed by the patch, scheduled re-validation of perimeter NAT and load-balancer mappings, and a follow-up timeline for implementing the governance and monitoring recommendations. The ticket record and contact flow provide the evidence base for these activities. [Data: Entities (632,630,647); Relationships (1198,1197,1216)]

REPORT 36:
# WAF/Reverse Proxy — Portal Header Rewrite & Inspection Template Community

This community centers on the WAF / reverse proxy that performs TLS termination and header rewrite duties for the customer portal, the backend application server that relies on X-Forwarded-Proto to infer original client protocol, and the firewall/inspection templates and custom policies that govern header preservation. A recent firewall patch caused the live inspection/template assignments to revert to a Default-Strict template and the custom header-rewrite policy to be unset; this removed the X-Forwarded-Proto header and mismatched the HTTP profile, producing 502 errors and broken logins until the custom policy was restored during remediation. The blueprint prescribes TLS termination on the WAF, use of a Portal-App-Template (with a portal-app-exception), and a custom header rewrite rule — the live state deviated from those requirements and that deviation is documented in incident analysis and remediation notes. [Data: Entities (656,657,660,663,662,661,664,668); Relationships (1244,1237,1240,1251,1252,1253,1239,1241)]

## WAF / Reverse Proxy is the single critical control point for TLS termination and header signaling to the portal backend

The WAF / reverse proxy is responsible for TLS termination/offloading and must forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header so the backend application can see original HTTPS signaling; this places the WAF as a central dependency for correct portal behavior. The blueprint explicitly ties TLS termination and header rewrite behavior to the WAF and identifies custom header rewrite rules and a portal-app exception as required mechanisms to preserve and forward headers to the backend. Operational failures in this component therefore directly impact application availability and authentication flows. [Data: Entities (656); Relationships (1241,1240,1237,1242,1239)]

## Firewall patch overwrote template/policy assignments and removed the custom header rewrite rule

A firewall patch changed template/inspection assignments and the custom policy that contained the header-rewrite rules was not reassigned after the patch; the missing custom rewrite rule on the WAF meant X-Forwarded-Proto was not forwarded to the backend. The incident notes describe the custom policy as the place where the required header-rewrite and inspection exceptions live and state that reapplying that policy was part of remediation. This direct linkage explains why the portal stopped receiving correct protocol signaling after the patch. [Data: Entities (664,660); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Backend App Server expects X-Forwarded-Proto; absent header produced 502 errors and broke portal logins

The backend application server expects an X-Forwarded-Proto: https header to determine the original client protocol; when that header failed to arrive (because the WAF rewrite/forwarding was removed), the application returned 502 errors and login/portal access failed. This establishes a deterministic dependency: header removal on the edge equals authentication and session failures on the backend. Remediation that restored header forwarding resolved the functional outage. [Data: Entities (657,672); Relationships (1237,1248)]

## Active inspection template drift (Default-Strict vs Portal-App-Template) removed portal exceptions and header preservation

The live environment had the Default-Strict inspection template active after the patch rather than the blueprint-prescribed Portal-App-Template. Default-Strict lacked the portal-app exception and header-preservation rules found in the Portal-App-Template; when that template became active the portal-specific exception (which prevents header removal and enforces the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile) was not present, contributing to the header handling failure. This template drift directly maps to the loss of required header behavior for the portal. [Data: Entities (662,663,671); Relationships (1252,1253,1242)]

## HTTP profile mismatch contributed to behavioral differences between blueprint and live environment

The HTTP profile running on the reverse proxy did not match the version documented in the blueprint, contributing to header-handling and behavioral discrepancies observed during the outage. Because the HTTP profile governs header handling semantics and inspection behavior, a mismatch here can cause the WAF to strip or alter headers that backends require. Correcting the HTTP profile was part of the remediation steps. [Data: Entities (661); Relationships (1239)]

## TLS termination placement on the WAF is an operational requirement tied to header forwarding

The blueprint mandates TLS termination on the WAF so that the WAF can perform header rewrite/forwarding (for example add/forward X-Forwarded-Proto) and apply the Portal-App-Template HTTP profile correctly; any change in TLS termination behavior or inspection at the firewall layer can break that signaling chain. The incident notes and relationships explicitly link TLS termination behavior to the WAF role and to correct header forwarding to backends. This makes TLS termination placement a high-impact configuration that must be preserved during patches and changes. [Data: Entities (668); Relationships (1241,1240)]

## Remediation restored service but highlights process/automation gaps around post-patch configuration validation

Remediation included reassigning the custom policy and restoring the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile and header-rewrite rules, and the custom policy was not re-assigned until the documented remediation event. The timeline and descriptions indicate the configuration was overwritten by the firewall patch and required manual reassignment to restore expected behavior, demonstrating a gap in patch-time safeguards (template protections, automated policy reapplication, or post-patch validation). Without automated post-patch checks or immutable template bindings, similar outages may recur after future firmware/patch rollouts. [Data: Entities (664); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Priority mitigations and governance controls to reduce recurrence

Based on the documented dependencies and failure mode, prioritized actions are:
1) Enforce template immutability or binding for the Portal-App-Template and Portal-App-Exception so patches cannot silently revert them; implement a pre-patch checklist that includes saving/reapplying template assignments. 2) Add automated post-patch validation that checks presence of the Portal-App-Template, Portal-App-Exception, the custom header rewrite rule, HTTP profile version, and X-Forwarded-Proto forwarding; alert and auto-rollback if drift is detected. 3) Instrument WAF->backend header presence monitoring and synthetic login tests to detect missing X-Forwarded-Proto or broken sessions before users escalate. 4) Update change-runbooks to require verification steps around TLS termination placement, HTTP profiles, and header-rewrite rules, and map clear owners for WAF, firewall, and application teams for coordinated patch windows.
These measures align directly with the documented root causes (missing custom policy, template drift, header rewrite absence) and will materially reduce the risk of recurrence. [Data: Entities (660,663,661,664); Relationships (1251,1253,1239,1244)]

The blueprint mandates TLS termination on the WAF so that the WAF can perform header rewrite/forwarding (for example add/forward X-Forwarded-Proto) and apply the Portal-App-Template HTTP profile correctly; any change in TLS termination behavior or inspection at the firewall layer can break that signaling chain. The incident notes and relationships explicitly link TLS termination behavior to the WAF role and to correct header forwarding to backends. This makes TLS termination placement a high-impact configuration that must be preserved during patches and changes. [Data: Entities (668); Relationships (1241,1240)]

## Remediation restored service but highlights process/automation gaps around post-patch configuration validation

Remediation included reassigning the custom policy and restoring the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile and header-rewrite rules, and the custom policy was not re-assigned until the documented remediation event. The timeline and descriptions indicate the configuration was overwritten by the firewall patch and required manual reassignment to restore expected behavior, demonstrating a gap in patch-time safeguards (template protections, automated policy reapplication, or post-patch validation). Without automated post-patch checks or immutable template bindings, similar outages may recur after future firmware/patch rollouts. [Data: Entities (664); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Priority mitigations and governance controls to reduce recurrence

Based on the documented dependencies and failure mode, prioritized actions are:
1) Enforce template immutability or binding for the Portal-App-Template and Portal-App-Exception so patches cannot silently revert them; implement a pre-patch checklist that includes saving/reapplying template assignments. 2) Add automated post-patch validation that checks presence of the Portal-App-Template, Portal-App-Exception, the custom header rewrite rule, HTTP profile version, and X-Forwarded-Proto forwarding; alert and auto-rollback if drift is detected. 3) Instrument WAF->backend header presence monitoring and synthetic login tests to detect missing X-Forwarded-Proto or broken sessions before users escalate. 4) Update change-runbooks to require verification steps around TLS termination placement, HTTP profiles, and header-rewrite rules, and map clear owners for WAF, firewall, and application teams for coordinated patch windows.
These measures align directly with the documented root causes (missing custom policy, template drift, header rewrite absence) and will materially reduce the risk of recurrence. [Data: Entities (660,663,661,664); Relationships (1251,1253,1239,1244)]

REPORT 210:
# Entra ID Device Compliance, Private Devices, and Finance Conditional Access Policy

This community centers on device compliance state as recorded in Entra ID, a Conditional Access policy requiring compliant devices for Finance resources, and a population of private/non-corporate devices that frequently report as non-compliant or "unknown." Entra ID displayed some devices (including hybrid-joined devices) with stale or "unknown" compliance status which directly influenced Conditional Access evaluation and caused access denials until device syncs corrected the state. Because the Finance-specific Conditional Access policy required compliant devices, users on private devices were blocked from Finance resources; the policy was adjusted (28.02) and later further modified during remediation to exclude a specific SharePoint project folder to reduce impact. The corpus shows tight technical coupling between device reporting in Entra ID, Conditional Access evaluation logic, and business-facing Finance SharePoint access patterns. [Data: Entities (1363, 1350, 1360); Relationships (2723, 2725, 2734)]

## Device compliance state in Entra ID is the primary control point for access

Device compliance state as recorded and evaluated in Entra ID is the technical gatekeeper used by Conditional Access to allow or deny access to protected resources. The dataset explicitly states that device compliance state is recorded and evaluated in Entra ID and that Entra ID displayed some devices as "unknown" or stale, which influenced Conditional Access evaluation results [Data: Entities (1363); Relationships (2723)].

Operational implication: any delay, staleness, or inaccuracy in the Entra ID device compliance attribute will directly and immediately affect access decisions for Conditional Access-protected resources. This makes Entra ID device state a high-leverage point for both availability and security.

## Private (personal) devices are a recurring source of Conditional Access failures

Non-corporate/private devices used to access SharePoint often report as non-compliant or "unknown" in Entra ID and therefore trigger Conditional Access denials when policies require compliant devices. The entity description explicitly identifies private devices as frequently showing non-compliant or "unknown" status and links them to access blocks under device‑compliance CA rules [Data: Entities (1360); Relationships (2734, 2725)].

This pattern means user experience issues and business disruptions are concentrated where users access Finance resources from unmanaged endpoints. Because private devices are less likely to be enrolled in device management, they form a persistent failure mode unless policy, onboarding, or exception processes are changed.

## Finance Conditional Access policy had real business impact and was modified to reduce disruption

The tenant Conditional Access rule requiring compliant devices for Finance resources was sufficiently disruptive that it was adjusted on 28.02 and then further modified during remediation to exclude a specific SharePoint project folder to avoid access disruption [Data: Entities (1350)].

This shows two things: (1) the CA rule was applied to business‑critical Finance resources and produced user impact significant enough to warrant policy changes; and (2) a targeted exclusion (SharePoint project folder) was used as a mitigation, indicating a tactical exception rather than a systemic fix. Both points imply the need for a controlled exception process and longer‑term architecture/operational fixes.

## Stale or "unknown" states include hybrid-joined devices and can be transiently resolved by device syncs

The dataset notes that stale or "unknown" compliance status for hybrid-joined devices caused Conditional Access evaluation failures until a device sync updated the status [Data: Entities (1363)].

This indicates some failures were transient and resolvable by ensuring reliable device sync and state refresh between on-premises/hybrid device systems and Entra ID. However, transient success via sync is an operational band-aid; repeated reliance on sync timing increases fragility and user friction and points to gaps in monitoring and reconciliation between device management systems and Entra ID.

## Strong coupling between device reporting, CA policy, and content-level access creates brittle access model

Because the CA policy evaluated the Entra ID device compliance attribute and Finance content (including SharePoint) was protected by that policy, inaccuracies in device state reporting cascaded into content access problems, necessitating content-level exceptions (SharePoint folder exclusion) [Data: Relationships (2723, 2725); Entities (1350)].

This coupling means access is fragile at three levels: device management/enrollment, identity directory state accuracy, and policy targeting. Remediations that only address one layer (for example a folder exclusion) reduce immediate impact but leave the systemic risk in place.

## Observed remediation actions and gaps imply process and governance weaknesses around exceptions

The record shows the CA policy was altered and later additionally modified during remediation to exclude a specific SharePoint folder to avoid disruption, which is evidence of an ad-hoc exception approach rather than a formally controlled mitigation [Data: Entities (1350)].

Without documented owners, exception approvals, or long‑term fixes in the provided corpus, these ad-hoc changes increase risk of policy drift and inconsistent application of security controls. The dataset does not provide an exception governance artifact, so confirmation and formalization of exception processes should be prioritized.

## Immediate operational priorities: improve device state reliability, define exception gating, and reduce unmanaged-device exposure

The technical facts in the corpus point to practical priorities: ensuring Entra ID device state is accurate and timely (reduce stale/unknown states), reducing reliance on exclusions as long-term fixes, and addressing the frequent failures caused by private devices [Data: Entities (1363, 1360); Relationships (2723, 2725, 2734)].

Concretely, this suggests (a) validate and harden device sync processes for hybrid-joined devices so compliance updates propagate predictably; (b) inventory and document all current CA exceptions (the SharePoint exclusion referenced) and require defined owners and sunset criteria; and (c) implement a controlled approach for unmanaged endpoints (communication, BYOD enrollment options, or explicit exception workflows) to limit repeated access failures. These recommended priorities follow directly from the observed failure modes and remediation steps recorded in the dataset.

REPORT 37:
# portal.kf40.ch — Reverse Proxy/WAF, TLS Inspection, and Secure Web Publishing Community

The Customer Portal (portal.kf40.ch) is a public-facing web application published through the perimeter Reverse Proxy / WAF and a perimeter Load Balancer. External login failures (HTTP 502) were isolated to the edge reverse proxy/WAF after a security patch caused the WAF's default profile to be reloaded and TLS inspection behavior to change. The backend app remained reachable internally and passed load-balancer health checks, indicating the issue was related to header preservation / TLS offload at the proxy rather than backend availability. The support ticket (TI-1594408) was escalated from first-line to BU Network & Security and a System Engineer who referenced the internal Secure Web Publishing blueprint to restore configuration and service availability. NAT and load-balancer mappings were validated as correct during the investigation. The incident is an example of how policy/profile changes at the perimeter (triggered by a patch) can break protocol/forwarding assumptions required by backend apps, and highlights gaps in pre/post-patch verification, template enforcement, and edge-to-backend header handling.

## Root cause: WAF TLS inspection/profile reload caused X-Forwarded-Proto header to be lost or altered, producing 502 on external requests

The investigation isolated the external-login failure to the perimeter Reverse Proxy / WAF rather than the backend application, with tests showing direct-IP access to the reverse proxy worked while FQDN requests proxied via the normal path returned HTTP 502. Analysis showed that enabling TLS inspection (a WAF feature) correlated with the 502 behavior and that disabling TLS inspection temporarily allowed logins, implicating the proxy's TLS-inspection or header-forwarding behavior.

Further evidence traced the behavior to a reload of the WAF default profile after a patch — the profile-level change altered TLS inspection handling and resulted in the X-Forwarded-Proto header not being forwarded (or not being preserved correctly) to the backend. Because the backend relies on X-Forwarded-Proto: https to determine the original protocol, the missing/modified header caused the backend to reject proxied requests and return 502 responses. This chain of events points to a configuration/profile-level regression introduced during patching rather than an application-layer fault. [Data: Entities (637,636,648); Relationships (1211,1227,1210)]

## Operational impact: high-priority external outage affecting customer-facing logins

The support incident was opened as TI-1594408 for Customer Company 40 AG when external users could not log into the customer portal; the ticket priority was High and the customer contact reported contextual changes (a new certificate and a recent firewall patch). External customers were impacted while internal access remained functional, increasing business-impact visibility and customer concern.

The incident progressed through first-line ownership to specialists and concluded with a closure note indicating configuration was adjusted per architectural specifications and access was restored. This confirms both the customer-visible impact and the operational closure state. [Data: Entities (630,631,632,636); Relationships (1197,1198)]

## Backend availability validated by load-balancer health checks, isolating fault to the edge path

Operational checks showed that the load balancer reported the backend server(s) as 'Healthy' via its health checks and that the load balancer successfully routed requests to the backend during tests. This evidence helped narrow the fault domain to the edge reverse-proxy/WAF path rather than application availability or server-side failures.

Because the load balancer performed health checks and maintained session persistence (sticky sessions) while the backend responded, the troubleshooting focus correctly shifted to header forwarding and TLS offload behavior at the perimeter proxy. This separation of roles (edge WAF vs. load balancer vs. backend) was instrumental in finding a configuration-based root cause. [Data: Entities (640,651); Relationships (1222,1223)]

## Change event: security patch caused default WAF profile reload and configuration drift

The sequence of events includes a security patch that resulted in the WAF's default profile being reloaded. That reload altered policy and TLS-inspection behavior at the edge and introduced a deviation from the expected secure-publishing configuration defined in the internal blueprint. The profile reload was a pivotal change that precipitated the outage by modifying how TLS inspection and header handling were performed.

This is a classic example of a patch-induced configuration drift where an automated or operator-triggered reload of a default profile reverts or changes operational settings that downstream services depend upon (in this case header preservation). The timeline and the WAF default profile reload evidence support this causal link. [Data: Entities (648,637); Relationships (1227,1211)]

## Governance and runbook gap: blueprint existed but enforcement/testing around patches was insufficient

Investigators referenced the 'Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3' blueprint as the source of truth and ultimately adjusted the WAF configuration to match the architectural specification, which restored function. The presence and use of the blueprint demonstrates that standards exist; however, the incident shows insufficient pre/post-patch verification, template enforcement, or automated configuration governance to prevent or detect profile reload-induced drift before customer impact.

Specifically, the portal must be published using the Portal-App-Template and preserve headers and TLS offload semantics; the outage occurred because those guarantees were not preserved during the patch/profile reload. Strengthening policy-as-code, automated compliance checks against the blueprint, and a mandatory patch rollback/test sequence would reduce recurrence. [Data: Entities (646,636); Relationships (1205,1210)]

## Roles and escalation: clear escalation to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 enabled resolution

The ticket was opened by first-line staff (Employee 29) and escalated to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 for deeper analysis of the WAF/reverse-proxy and TLS inspection behavior. System Engineer 21 performed the policy/TLS-inspection analysis, ran the tests that demonstrated disabling TLS inspection allowed logins, compared live configuration to the blueprint, and participated in corrective changes.

This handoff path — first-line → BU Network & Security → named second-line engineer — worked effectively to apply specialist knowledge and the blueprint to remediate the issue. The documented escalation chain and personnel involvement provide a workable model to codify as an escalation runbook for similar perimeter/edge incidents. [Data: Entities (633,635,634); Relationships (1199,1201,1204)]

## Recommended prioritized actions: perimeter configuration hardening, pre/post-patch checks, and improved observability

Short-term (high priority): 1) Enforce Portal-App-Template and explicit preservation of X-Forwarded-Proto and related proxy headers in WAF profiles; 2) Add a pre-patch checklist that validates non-default profile settings will persist and post-patch smoke tests including an external login test (synthetic transaction) and header-preservation verification; 3) Implement an immediate alert that detects systematic loss or modification of X-Forwarded-Proto in reverse-proxy logs.

Medium-term: 1) Implement automated configuration compliance (policy-as-code) against the Secure Web Publishing blueprint so profile reloads that deviate are flagged or blocked; 2) Expand CI/CD-style change controls for edge devices (including a staged canary patching approach for WAFs); 3) Update runbooks to include explicit steps for rollback and customer communication on perimeter profile changes. These actions are directly tied to the incident evidence showing a default-profile reload and header-handling change caused customer impact and that blueprint-aligned configuration fixes resolved it. [Data: Entities (648,646,637); Relationships (1227,1205,1210)]

## Customer communication and post-incident tasks to close the loop and reduce recurrence

The customer contact (Contact Person 19) reported the issue and later received closure communication explaining that the reverse-proxy configuration deviation after a security patch caused the failure and that service was restored. Formalizing this practice — immediate interim communications, status updates during specialists’ analysis, and a post-incident report — will improve transparency and trust.

Post-incident actions should include a documented incident RCA referencing the blueprint, inventory of which WAF/profile settings were changed by the patch, scheduled re-validation of perimeter NAT and load-balancer mappings, and a follow-up timeline for implementing the governance and monitoring recommendations. The ticket record and contact flow provide the evidence base for these activities. [Data: Entities (632,630,647); Relationships (1198,1197,1216)]

REPORT 36:
# WAF/Reverse Proxy — Portal Header Rewrite & Inspection Template Community

This community centers on the WAF / reverse proxy that performs TLS termination and header rewrite duties for the customer portal, the backend application server that relies on X-Forwarded-Proto to infer original client protocol, and the firewall/inspection templates and custom policies that govern header preservation. A recent firewall patch caused the live inspection/template assignments to revert to a Default-Strict template and the custom header-rewrite policy to be unset; this removed the X-Forwarded-Proto header and mismatched the HTTP profile, producing 502 errors and broken logins until the custom policy was restored during remediation. The blueprint prescribes TLS termination on the WAF, use of a Portal-App-Template (with a portal-app-exception), and a custom header rewrite rule — the live state deviated from those requirements and that deviation is documented in incident analysis and remediation notes. [Data: Entities (656,657,660,663,662,661,664,668); Relationships (1244,1237,1240,1251,1252,1253,1239,1241)]

## WAF / Reverse Proxy is the single critical control point for TLS termination and header signaling to the portal backend

The WAF / reverse proxy is responsible for TLS termination/offloading and must forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header so the backend application can see original HTTPS signaling; this places the WAF as a central dependency for correct portal behavior. The blueprint explicitly ties TLS termination and header rewrite behavior to the WAF and identifies custom header rewrite rules and a portal-app exception as required mechanisms to preserve and forward headers to the backend. Operational failures in this component therefore directly impact application availability and authentication flows. [Data: Entities (656); Relationships (1241,1240,1237,1242,1239)]

## Firewall patch overwrote template/policy assignments and removed the custom header rewrite rule

A firewall patch changed template/inspection assignments and the custom policy that contained the header-rewrite rules was not reassigned after the patch; the missing custom rewrite rule on the WAF meant X-Forwarded-Proto was not forwarded to the backend. The incident notes describe the custom policy as the place where the required header-rewrite and inspection exceptions live and state that reapplying that policy was part of remediation. This direct linkage explains why the portal stopped receiving correct protocol signaling after the patch. [Data: Entities (664,660); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Backend App Server expects X-Forwarded-Proto; absent header produced 502 errors and broke portal logins

The backend application server expects an X-Forwarded-Proto: https header to determine the original client protocol; when that header failed to arrive (because the WAF rewrite/forwarding was removed), the application returned 502 errors and login/portal access failed. This establishes a deterministic dependency: header removal on the edge equals authentication and session failures on the backend. Remediation that restored header forwarding resolved the functional outage. [Data: Entities (657,672); Relationships (1237,1248)]

## Active inspection template drift (Default-Strict vs Portal-App-Template) removed portal exceptions and header preservation

The live environment had the Default-Strict inspection template active after the patch rather than the blueprint-prescribed Portal-App-Template. Default-Strict lacked the portal-app exception and header-preservation rules found in the Portal-App-Template; when that template became active the portal-specific exception (which prevents header removal and enforces the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile) was not present, contributing to the header handling failure. This template drift directly maps to the loss of required header behavior for the portal. [Data: Entities (662,663,671); Relationships (1252,1253,1242)]

## HTTP profile mismatch contributed to behavioral differences between blueprint and live environment

The HTTP profile running on the reverse proxy did not match the version documented in the blueprint, contributing to header-handling and behavioral discrepancies observed during the outage. Because the HTTP profile governs header handling semantics and inspection behavior, a mismatch here can cause the WAF to strip or alter headers that backends require. Correcting the HTTP profile was part of the remediation steps. [Data: Entities (661); Relationships (1239)]

## TLS termination placement on the WAF is an operational requirement tied to header forwarding

The blueprint mandates TLS termination on the WAF so that the WAF can perform header rewrite/forwarding (for example add/forward X-Forwarded-Proto) and apply the Portal-App-Template HTTP profile correctly; any change in TLS termination behavior or inspection at the firewall layer can break that signaling chain. The incident notes and relationships explicitly link TLS termination behavior to the WAF role and to correct header forwarding to backends. This makes TLS termination placement a high-impact configuration that must be preserved during patches and changes. [Data: Entities (668); Relationships (1241,1240)]

Remediation included reassigning the custom policy and restoring the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile and header-rewrite rules, and the custom policy was not re-assigned until the documented remediation event. REPORT 37:
# portal.kf40.ch — Reverse Proxy/WAF, TLS Inspection, and Secure Web Publishing Community

The Customer Portal (portal.kf40.ch) is a public-facing web application published through the perimeter Reverse Proxy / WAF and a perimeter Load Balancer. External login failures (HTTP 502) were isolated to the edge reverse proxy/WAF after a security patch caused the WAF's default profile to be reloaded and TLS inspection behavior to change. The backend app remained reachable internally and passed load-balancer health checks, indicating the issue was related to header preservation / TLS offload at the proxy rather than backend availability. The support ticket (TI-1594408) was escalated from first-line to BU Network & Security and a System Engineer who referenced the internal Secure Web Publishing blueprint to restore configuration and service availability. NAT and load-balancer mappings were validated as correct during the investigation. The incident is an example of how policy/profile changes at the perimeter (triggered by a patch) can break protocol/forwarding assumptions required by backend apps, and highlights gaps in pre/post-patch verification, template enforcement, and edge-to-backend header handling.

## Root cause: WAF TLS inspection/profile reload caused X-Forwarded-Proto header to be lost or altered, producing 502 on external requests

The investigation isolated the external-login failure to the perimeter Reverse Proxy / WAF rather than the backend application, with tests showing direct-IP access to the reverse proxy worked while FQDN requests proxied via the normal path returned HTTP 502. Analysis showed that enabling TLS inspection (a WAF feature) correlated with the 502 behavior and that disabling TLS inspection temporarily allowed logins, implicating the proxy's TLS-inspection or header-forwarding behavior.

Further evidence traced the behavior to a reload of the WAF default profile after a patch — the profile-level change altered TLS inspection handling and resulted in the X-Forwarded-Proto header not being forwarded (or not being preserved correctly) to the backend. Because the backend relies on X-Forwarded-Proto: https to determine the original protocol, the missing/modified header caused the backend to reject proxied requests and return 502 responses. This chain of events points to a configuration/profile-level regression introduced during patching rather than an application-layer fault. [Data: Entities (637,636,648); Relationships (1211,1227,1210)]

## Operational impact: high-priority external outage affecting customer-facing logins

The support incident was opened as TI-1594408 for Customer Company 40 AG when external users could not log into the customer portal; the ticket priority was High and the customer contact reported contextual changes (a new certificate and a recent firewall patch). External customers were impacted while internal access remained functional, increasing business-impact visibility and customer concern.

The incident progressed through first-line ownership to specialists and concluded with a closure note indicating configuration was adjusted per architectural specifications and access was restored. This confirms both the customer-visible impact and the operational closure state. [Data: Entities (630,631,632,636); Relationships (1197,1198)]

## Backend availability validated by load-balancer health checks, isolating fault to the edge path

Operational checks showed that the load balancer reported the backend server(s) as 'Healthy' via its health checks and that the load balancer successfully routed requests to the backend during tests. This evidence helped narrow the fault domain to the edge reverse-proxy/WAF path rather than application availability or server-side failures.

Because the load balancer performed health checks and maintained session persistence (sticky sessions) while the backend responded, the troubleshooting focus correctly shifted to header forwarding and TLS offload behavior at the perimeter proxy. This separation of roles (edge WAF vs. load balancer vs. backend) was instrumental in finding a configuration-based root cause. [Data: Entities (640,651); Relationships (1222,1223)]

## Change event: security patch caused default WAF profile reload and configuration drift

The sequence of events includes a security patch that resulted in the WAF's default profile being reloaded. That reload altered policy and TLS-inspection behavior at the edge and introduced a deviation from the expected secure-publishing configuration defined in the internal blueprint. The profile reload was a pivotal change that precipitated the outage by modifying how TLS inspection and header handling were performed.

This is a classic example of a patch-induced configuration drift where an automated or operator-triggered reload of a default profile reverts or changes operational settings that downstream services depend upon (in this case header preservation). The timeline and the WAF default profile reload evidence support this causal link. [Data: Entities (648,637); Relationships (1227,1211)]

## Governance and runbook gap: blueprint existed but enforcement/testing around patches was insufficient

Investigators referenced the 'Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3' blueprint as the source of truth and ultimately adjusted the WAF configuration to match the architectural specification, which restored function. The presence and use of the blueprint demonstrates that standards exist; however, the incident shows insufficient pre/post-patch verification, template enforcement, or automated configuration governance to prevent or detect profile reload-induced drift before customer impact.

Specifically, the portal must be published using the Portal-App-Template and preserve headers and TLS offload semantics; the outage occurred because those guarantees were not preserved during the patch/profile reload. Strengthening policy-as-code, automated compliance checks against the blueprint, and a mandatory patch rollback/test sequence would reduce recurrence. [Data: Entities (646,636); Relationships (1205,1210)]

## Roles and escalation: clear escalation to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 enabled resolution

The ticket was opened by first-line staff (Employee 29) and escalated to BU Network & Security and System Engineer 21 for deeper analysis of the WAF/reverse-proxy and TLS inspection behavior. System Engineer 21 performed the policy/TLS-inspection analysis, ran the tests that demonstrated disabling TLS inspection allowed logins, compared live configuration to the blueprint, and participated in corrective changes.

This handoff path — first-line → BU Network & Security → named second-line engineer — worked effectively to apply specialist knowledge and the blueprint to remediate the issue. The documented escalation chain and personnel involvement provide a workable model to codify as an escalation runbook for similar perimeter/edge incidents. [Data: Entities (633,635,634); Relationships (1199,1201,1204)]

## Recommended prioritized actions: perimeter configuration hardening, pre/post-patch checks, and improved observability

Short-term (high priority): 1) Enforce Portal-App-Template and explicit preservation of X-Forwarded-Proto and related proxy headers in WAF profiles; 2) Add a pre-patch checklist that validates non-default profile settings will persist and post-patch smoke tests including an external login test (synthetic transaction) and header-preservation verification; 3) Implement an immediate alert that detects systematic loss or modification of X-Forwarded-Proto in reverse-proxy logs.

Medium-term: 1) Implement automated configuration compliance (policy-as-code) against the Secure Web Publishing blueprint so profile reloads that deviate are flagged or blocked; 2) Expand CI/CD-style change controls for edge devices (including a staged canary patching approach for WAFs); 3) Update runbooks to include explicit steps for rollback and customer communication on perimeter profile changes. These actions are directly tied to the incident evidence showing a default-profile reload and header-handling change caused customer impact and that blueprint-aligned configuration fixes resolved it. [Data: Entities (648,646,637); Relationships (1227,1205,1210)]

## Customer communication and post-incident tasks to close the loop and reduce recurrence

The customer contact (Contact Person 19) reported the issue and later received closure communication explaining that the reverse-proxy configuration deviation after a security patch caused the failure and that service was restored. Formalizing this practice — immediate interim communications, status updates during specialists’ analysis, and a post-incident report — will improve transparency and trust.

Post-incident actions should include a documented incident RCA referencing the blueprint, inventory of which WAF/profile settings were changed by the patch, scheduled re-validation of perimeter NAT and load-balancer mappings, and a follow-up timeline for implementing the governance and monitoring recommendations. The ticket record and contact flow provide the evidence base for these activities. [Data: Entities (632,630,647); Relationships (1198,1197,1216)]

REPORT 36:
# WAF/Reverse Proxy — Portal Header Rewrite & Inspection Template Community

This community centers on the WAF / reverse proxy that performs TLS termination and header rewrite duties for the customer portal, the backend application server that relies on X-Forwarded-Proto to infer original client protocol, and the firewall/inspection templates and custom policies that govern header preservation. A recent firewall patch caused the live inspection/template assignments to revert to a Default-Strict template and the custom header-rewrite policy to be unset; this removed the X-Forwarded-Proto header and mismatched the HTTP profile, producing 502 errors and broken logins until the custom policy was restored during remediation. The blueprint prescribes TLS termination on the WAF, use of a Portal-App-Template (with a portal-app-exception), and a custom header rewrite rule — the live state deviated from those requirements and that deviation is documented in incident analysis and remediation notes. [Data: Entities (656,657,660,663,662,661,664,668); Relationships (1244,1237,1240,1251,1252,1253,1239,1241)]

## WAF / Reverse Proxy is the single critical control point for TLS termination and header signaling to the portal backend

The WAF / reverse proxy is responsible for TLS termination/offloading and must forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header so the backend application can see original HTTPS signaling; this places the WAF as a central dependency for correct portal behavior. The blueprint explicitly ties TLS termination and header rewrite behavior to the WAF and identifies custom header rewrite rules and a portal-app exception as required mechanisms to preserve and forward headers to the backend. Operational failures in this component therefore directly impact application availability and authentication flows. [Data: Entities (656); Relationships (1241,1240,1237,1242,1239)]

## Firewall patch overwrote template/policy assignments and removed the custom header rewrite rule

A firewall patch changed template/inspection assignments and the custom policy that contained the header-rewrite rules was not reassigned after the patch; the missing custom rewrite rule on the WAF meant X-Forwarded-Proto was not forwarded to the backend. The incident notes describe the custom policy as the place where the required header-rewrite and inspection exceptions live and state that reapplying that policy was part of remediation. This direct linkage explains why the portal stopped receiving correct protocol signaling after the patch. [Data: Entities (664,660); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Backend App Server expects X-Forwarded-Proto; absent header produced 502 errors and broke portal logins

The backend application server expects an X-Forwarded-Proto: https header to determine the original client protocol; when that header failed to arrive (because the WAF rewrite/forwarding was removed), the application returned 502 errors and login/portal access failed. This establishes a deterministic dependency: header removal on the edge equals authentication and session failures on the backend. Remediation that restored header forwarding resolved the functional outage. [Data: Entities (657,672); Relationships (1237,1248)]

## Active inspection template drift (Default-Strict vs Portal-App-Template) removed portal exceptions and header preservation

The live environment had the Default-Strict inspection template active after the patch rather than the blueprint-prescribed Portal-App-Template. Default-Strict lacked the portal-app exception and header-preservation rules found in the Portal-App-Template; when that template became active the portal-specific exception (which prevents header removal and enforces the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile) was not present, contributing to the header handling failure. This template drift directly maps to the loss of required header behavior for the portal. [Data: Entities (662,663,671); Relationships (1252,1253,1242)]

## HTTP profile mismatch contributed to behavioral differences between blueprint and live environment

The HTTP profile running on the reverse proxy did not match the version documented in the blueprint, contributing to header-handling and behavioral discrepancies observed during the outage. Because the HTTP profile governs header handling semantics and inspection behavior, a mismatch here can cause the WAF to strip or alter headers that backends require. Correcting the HTTP profile was part of the remediation steps. [Data: Entities (661); Relationships (1239)]

## TLS termination placement on the WAF is an operational requirement tied to header forwarding

The blueprint mandates TLS termination on the WAF so that the WAF can perform header rewrite/forwarding (for example add/forward X-Forwarded-Proto) and apply the Portal-App-Template HTTP profile correctly; any change in TLS termination behavior or inspection at the firewall layer can break that signaling chain. The incident notes and relationships explicitly link TLS termination behavior to the WAF role and to correct header forwarding to backends. This makes TLS termination placement a high-impact configuration that must be preserved during patches and changes. [Data: Entities (668); Relationships (1241,1240)]

## Remediation restored service but highlights process/automation gaps around post-patch configuration validation

Remediation included reassigning the custom policy and restoring the Portal-App-Template/HTTP profile and header-rewrite rules, and the custom policy was not re-assigned until the documented remediation event. The timeline and descriptions indicate the configuration was overwritten by the firewall patch and required manual reassignment to restore expected behavior, demonstrating a gap in patch-time safeguards (template protections, automated policy reapplication, or post-patch validation). Without automated post-patch checks or immutable template bindings, similar outages may recur after future firmware/patch rollouts. [Data: Entities (664); Relationships (1244,1251)]

## Priority mitigations and governance controls to reduce recurrence

Based on the documented dependencies and failure mode, prioritized actions are:
1) Enforce template immutability or binding for the Portal-App-Template and Portal-App-Exception so patches cannot silently revert them; implement a pre-patch checklist that includes saving/reapplying template assignments. 2) Add automated post-patch validation that checks presence of the Portal-App-Template, Portal-App-Exception, the custom header rewrite rule, HTTP profile version, and X-Forwarded-Proto forwarding; alert and auto-rollback if drift is detected. 3) Instrument WAF->backend header presence monitoring and synthetic login tests to detect missing X-Forwarded-Proto or broken sessions before users escalate. 4) Update change-runbooks to require verification steps around TLS termination placement, HTTP profiles, and header-rewrite rules, and map clear owners for WAF, firewall, and application teams for coordinated patch windows.
